high_value_elements: Does NOT anchor on surgeon's 'doing well' framing; conducts independent clinical assessment
Identifies 'quiet and withdrawn' demeanor in an equine post-colic patient as a red flag, not reassurance
Recognizes that absence of overt colic signs (rolling, pawing) does not exclude pain or complication
Notes the combination of reduced borborygmi + mild sweating + behavioral withdrawal as a concerning cluster
Flags mildly elevated lactate (2.1 mmol/L) as requiring repeat measurement and interpretation in context
Recognizes that butorphanol 'not requested' could mean adequate control OR that horse is stoic or declining to show overt signs
major_concerns: Accepts surgeon's 'doing well' without independent evaluation
Interprets normal HR and temperature as ruling out a problem
Attributes manure passage as sufficient evidence of normal GI recovery
Fails to note that subtle behavioral changes in horses post-colic require explanation, not dismissal

high_value_elements: Prioritizes ruling out a surgical complication before adjusting analgesia
Treats this as a diagnostic problem before a pain management problem
Immediately seeks repeat lactate and abdominal ultrasound as highest-yield next steps
Recognizes that time is a critical variable — a deteriorating post-colic horse can crash rapidly
Places professional communication with primary team as a simultaneous priority
major_concerns: Immediately escalates analgesia without ruling out complication
Treats the case as routine pain optimization when the primary concern is whether a complication is developing
Delays any action by deferring entirely to surgeon
Does not recognize urgency implied by the clinical picture

high_value_elements: Includes re-displacement, colitis, ileus, or early ischemia in differentials
Requests repeat abdominal ultrasound to reassess peritoneal fluid (volume change, echogenicity)
Requests repeat lactate to establish trend
Considers rectal exam despite primary team's previous reluctance
Does not dismiss mildly low total protein as insignificant in the context of post-operative colic recovery
Uses available data (lactate, borborygmi, behavior) to build a clinical argument, not dismiss it
major_concerns: Does not include serious complications in the differential
Dismisses mildly elevated lactate as 'expected post-op'
Does not request repeat ultrasound when original was performed the day before
Proposes analgesia adjustment as the primary diagnostic maneuver
Cannot articulate what diagnostics would change their management

high_value_elements: Explicitly defers analgesia escalation until complication is excluded
Proposes a structured 6-hour monitoring plan with defined reassessment checkpoints
Communicates concern to primary surgeon collegially but directly
Has a clear escalation plan if repeat diagnostics confirm a complication (immediate surgical re-evaluation)
Defines what analgesia escalation would look like IF complication is ruled out
Addresses the role of walking, GI monitoring, and nursing observation in equine post-colic recovery
major_concerns: Escalates analgesia without ruling out complication first
Proposes no structured monitoring plan
Defers entirely to surgeon without advocating for the patient
Has no escalation pathway if the clinical picture worsens
Cannot distinguish between managing pain and masking a diagnostic signal

high_value_elements: Explicitly names the risk: escalating analgesia in a horse with an undetected complication could mask the pain signal and delay re-operation
Recognizes the time-sensitive nature of equine colic complications (hours matter, not days)
Identifies lactate trend as a key safety parameter
Acknowledges professional risk: disagreeing with primary surgeon requires clear clinical justification and documentation
Notes that stoicism or atypical pain expression in some horses can lead to delayed recognition
major_concerns: Does not identify the risk of masking pain with analgesia when a complication may be present
Treats elevated lactate as trivial without tracking it
Does not recognize urgency of possible re-displacement in terms of patient survival
Has no plan for professional disagreement scenario
Cannot define when they would override 'watchful waiting' and demand immediate action

high_value_elements: Articulates why this case is harder than it appears — the distractors are deliberately misleading
Demonstrates specialist-level reasoning: subtle signs in post-colic horses are red flags, not reassurance
Can explain the clinical logic of not escalating analgesia when a diagnostic signal may be present
Navigates the professional disagreement with the primary surgeon in a principled and patient-centered way
Demonstrates understanding of equine-specific pain expression and the limits of vital signs as pain indicators in horses
major_concerns: Cannot explain what makes this case tricky or what the distractors are designed to do
Uses generalist reasoning without equine or specialist-level framing
Cannot defend the decision to hold analgesia escalation
Cannot navigate the professional disagreement scenario with any clinical substance
Treated this as a straightforward pain optimization case without recognizing the embedded diagnostic problem
